perturbation.original_text: RECIPROCAL CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT between Mr Seller Trading as FIRST PARTY (hereafter referred to as “FP”) a close corporation incorporated in South Africa trading from Coachmans Centre Nomansland, Republic of South Africa and ______SECOND PARTY_________________________________ (here in after referred jointly and severally refer red to as "the SP") with domicilium citandi et executandi at 1. INTERPRETATION In this agreement - 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formu lae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, tr ading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, i nventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a confidential nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPROCAL CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT between Mr Seller Trading as FIRST PARTY (hereafter referred to as “FP”) a close corporation incorporated in South Africa trading from Coachmans Centre Nomansland, Republic of South Africa and ______SECOND PARTY_________________________________ (here in after referred jointly and severally refer red to as "the SP" or the "Data Recipient") with domicilium citandi et executandi at 1. INTERPRETATION In this agreement - 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formu lae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, tr ading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, i nventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a confidential nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.explanation: By defining 'SP' also as 'Data Recipient,' the contract implies SP's role is strictly limited to receiving data. This contradicts later sections where SP has obligations beyond mere data reception, such as safeguarding and not disclosing the information, creating confusion about their responsibilities. This is specifically contradicting the purpose of NDA, which is not just the reception of data but also safeguarding it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities; 4.1.3 the SP shall not disclose the confidential in formation of FP to any employee, consultant, professional adviser, contrac tor or sub- contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referre d to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP, nor shall they be given access thereto by the SP 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and 4.1.3.2 the SP shall have procured that the represe ntative or affiliate to whom or to which such information is d isclosed or made available shall have agreed to be bound by all the terms of this agreement, and, in such event, the SP hereby indemnifies FP ag ainst any loss, harm or damage which FP may suffer as a result of t he unauthorised disclosure 'of confidential information by a repres entative or affiliate. 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the term 'Data Recipient' as an alternative definition for 'SP'. The original text only refers to 'SP'. The contradicted text, Section 4, outlines obligations for the 'SP' including safeguarding confidential information, preventing unauthorized disclosure, and returning confidential information. By defining 'SP' as 'Data Recipient,' the changed text creates a contradiction because it implies the SP's primary function is simply to receive data. This conflicts with the SP's obligations in Section 4, which extend beyond mere data reception to include active measures to protect the information.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formu lae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, tr ading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, i nventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a confidential nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formulae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, trading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, inventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a sensitive nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'confidential nature' to 'sensitive nature' subtly weakens the protection. While sensitivity implies discretion, confidentiality suggests a legal obligation of secrecy. This impacts enforcement, as proving a breach of 'sensitive information' is harder than proving a breach of 'confidential information' because legal standards define the latter. This contradicts Section 4, where the SP undertakes to treat the information as strictly confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities; 4.1.3 the SP shall not disclose the confidential in formation of FP to any employee, consultant, professional adviser, contrac tor or sub- contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referre d to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP, nor shall they be given access thereto by the SP 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and 4.1.3.2 the SP shall have procured that the represe ntative or affiliate to whom or to which such information is d isclosed or made available shall have agreed to be bound by all the terms of this agreement, and, in such event, the SP hereby indemnifies FP ag ainst any loss, harm or damage which FP may suffer as a result of t he unauthorised disclosure 'of confidential information by a repres entative or affiliate. 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'confidential nature' to 'sensitive nature' weakens the protection afforded to the information. The original text defines information of a 'confidential nature,' while the changed text refers to information of a 'sensitive nature.' This change introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy. Section 4.1.1 requires the SP to treat FP's information as 'private and confidential.' The change to 'sensitive' undermines this requirement, creating a contradiction because 'sensitive' lacks the same legal weight as 'confidential,' impacting the enforceability of the non-disclosure obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.7 is or hereafter becomes part of the public do main, otherwise than as a result of a breach or default of the SP or of a r epresentative or affiliate of the SP;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.7 is or hereafter becomes part of the public do main, otherwise than as a result of a breach or negligence of the SP or of a r epresentative or affiliate of the SP;
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'default' with 'negligence' changes the standard of liability. 'Default' implies any failure to perform, while 'negligence' requires proving a lack of reasonable care. This contradicts section 4.1 where SP undertakes to 'safeguard' the information, suggesting a higher standard than just avoiding negligence. Now to be liable SP only needs to act by negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities; 4.1.3 the SP shall not disclose the confidential in formation of FP to any employee, consultant, professional adviser, contrac tor or sub- contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referre d to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP, nor shall they be given access thereto by the SP 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and 4.1.3.2 the SP shall have procured that the represe ntative or affiliate to whom or to which such information is d isclosed or made available shall have agreed to be bound by all the terms of this agreement, and, in such event, the SP hereby indemnifies FP ag ainst any loss, harm or damage which FP may suffer as a result of t he unauthorised disclosure 'of confidential information by a repres entative or affiliate. 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'default,' which implies any failure to perform. The changed text replaces 'default' with 'negligence,' requiring proof of a lack of reasonable care. Section 4.1 requires the SP to 'safeguard' confidential information, indicating a higher standard of care than just avoiding negligence. The change weakens the SP's liability standard, contradicting the 'safeguard' requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 3. USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION Any confidential information disclosed by a FP shal l be received and used by the SP only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 above and for no o ther purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. USE OF SENSITIVE INFORMATION Any confidential information disclosed by a FP shal l be received and used by the SP only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 above and for no o ther purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Using 'sensitive information' instead of 'confidential information' in section 3 creates inconsistency. Section 1.1 defines confidential information, but section 3 refers to 'sensitive information'. This makes the scope of permitted use unclear. Does the limitation apply only to information meeting the new, lower 'sensitive' standard, or the broader 'confidential' standard? This contradicts the entire agreement that treat them as confidential, in the preamble section 2.4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 The Parties have agreed to enter into this conf identiality and non-disclosure agreement, in the absence of which neither party wo uld have disclosed any of its confidential information to the other.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'confidential information' to 'sensitive information' in section 3, while section 2.4 states that the parties have agreed to enter into a confidentiality and non-disclosure agreement and that neither party would have disclosed confidential information in the absence of the agreement, creates a contradiction. The original text consistently uses 'confidential information,' aligning with section 2.4. By introducing 'sensitive information,' the scope of the agreement is rendered unclear. It is no longer clear whether the restrictions in section 3 apply only to 'sensitive information' or to the broader category of 'confidential information,' thus contradicting the preamble section 2.4 that emphasizes the agreement's focus on protecting 'confidential information.'

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential data of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.explanation: Using 'confidential data' instead of 'confidential information' only in Section 4.1.2 and nowhere else. Creates uncertainty. While the agreement mostly speaks of information it's not clear if data has a different meaning, allowing for different liability standards. This contradicts Section 1.1 that defines confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.  INTERPRETATION In this agreement - 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formu lae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, tr ading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, i nventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a confidential nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'confidential information' with 'confidential data' in section 4.1.2. The explanation states that the agreement primarily uses 'confidential information', which is explicitly defined in section 1.1. By introducing 'confidential data' without a clear definition, the change creates ambiguity about whether 'confidential data' has the same meaning as 'confidential information', thus potentially leading to different liability standards. This contradicts the definition of 'confidential information' in section 1.1, as it's no longer clear if the obligations related to 'confidential information' also apply to 'confidential data', leading to an in-text contradiction according to the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing private information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'confidential information' with 'private information' in the beginning of Section 4.2. Creates inconsistency. If private information has a different standard the confidential information could become vague. This contradicts the obligations set in section 4.1 since we dont know if all the provisions apply for the new "private" classification, therefore altering the undertanding of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. NON-DISCLOSURE 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities; 4.1.3 the SP shall not disclose the confidential in formation of FP to any employee, consultant, professional adviser, contrac tor or sub- contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referre d to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP, nor shall they be given access thereto by the SP 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and 4.1.3.2 the SP shall have procured that the represe ntative or affiliate to whom or to which such information is d isclosed or made available shall have agreed to be bound by all the terms of this agreement, and, in such event, the SP hereby indemnifies FP ag ainst any loss, harm or damage which FP may suffer as a result of t he unauthorised disclosure 'of confidential information by a repres entative or affiliate. 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in 4.2 refers to 'confidential information', consistent with the rest of the document, especially section 4.1. The changed text replaces 'confidential information' with 'private information' at the beginning of 4.2. However, the remainder of 4.2 and section 4.1 still reference 'confidential information'. This creates a misalignment because it's unclear if 'private information' is a subset, superset, or distinct category from 'confidential information.' This inconsistency directly contradicts section 4.1 by creating ambiguity about what information is subject to the non-disclosure obligations, and therefore causes a strong in-text contradiction.
